# WebForge — Full Reference

> WebForge is a free, AI-ready website capture suite available online, as a Chrome extension, and as Python/npm CLI packages. It turns any website into a structured, code-ready visual blueprint (multi-viewport screenshots, domain crawl sitemaps, and CSS design tokens) so AI coding agents like Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT and Gemini can reconstruct a frontend with high fidelity.

- Homepage: https://webforge.theatom.lk
- Install / Source code: https://github.com/zakisheriff/WebForge
- Releases: https://github.com/zakisheriff/WebForge/releases
- Author: Zaki Sheriff — https://github.com/zakisheriff
- Publisher: The Atom
- Price: Free
- Platform: Web (Online preview), Chrome Extension, Python Library (Playwright), npm Package (Puppeteer)

## What problem it solves

AI coding agents recreate frontend layouts far more accurately when given clear visual references and exact design tokens. Pasting a single screenshot loses responsive behavior and precise colors/fonts. WebForge automates the full capture: it scrolls and stitches each page, emulates device viewports, crawls the whole domain, and packages everything an agent needs.

## Online capture tool (no install required)

The WebForge website hosts a free browser-based tool at https://webforge.theatom.lk/#try. You paste any website URL and it renders the page server-side across three viewports (desktop 1440, tablet 768, mobile 390), takes full-page screenshots, and extracts the color palette, fonts and image URLs — all downloadable as a ZIP with a metadata.json. This is a single-page preview of the extension: it captures one page per run and cannot crawl a whole domain or reach login/bot-protected pages. For full-domain crawling and pixel-perfect fidelity, install the Chrome extension.

## Capture modes

- **Single page** — capture the current page as a stitched full-page screenshot.
- **Multi-viewport** — capture the same page at desktop, tablet and mobile widths to reveal responsive/media-query behavior.
- **Full-domain crawl** — discover and capture same-domain pages sequentially, with a configurable page limit (2–20).
- **Design tokens only** — extract colors and fonts without full captures.

## How it works (step by step)

1. Install WebForge from GitHub into a Chrome/Chromium browser.
2. Open the target website and click the WebForge toolbar popup.
3. Pick a capture mode (single, multi-viewport, or full-domain crawl) and optionally set custom crawl URLs and a page limit.
4. WebForge scrolls each page, hides scrollbars and sticky headers, waits out lazy-loading, and stitches a seamless canvas per viewport.
5. It extracts the active color palette (as hex) and font families.
6. It compiles a ZIP blueprint package and downloads it, ready to feed to an AI coding agent.

## Technical details

- **Incremental stitching**: scrolls dynamic pages, bypasses lazy-loading delays, hides scrollbars, and hides fixed/sticky headers to avoid duplicated overlays.
- **Viewport emulation dimensions**: Desktop 1440x900, Tablet 768x1024, Mobile 390x844.
- **Domain crawler**: spawns temporary sandboxed tabs to crawl and capture same-domain URLs sequentially.
- **Token extractor**: harvests the document's computed color palette (converted to hex) and font-family list.

## Output package schema

Every exported ZIP is a directory:

```
WebForge_Project/
├── pages/
│   ├── home/
│   │   ├── desktop.png        (1440x900 full page)
│   │   ├── tablet.png         (768x1024 full page)
│   │   ├── mobile.png         (390x844 full page)
│   │   └── metadata.json      (page title, URL, fonts, colors)
│   └── about/
│       └── ...
├── sitemap.json               (array of crawled URLs / domain tree)
└── metadata.json              (global fonts, colors, timestamps, page index)
```

## Using a blueprint inside an AI agent

- Feed the global `metadata.json` first to establish the design system (fonts, colors).
- Attach the relevant device PNG from `pages/<slug>/` as the visual target.
- Use the extracted hex colors for exact color matching rather than eyeballing screenshots.
- Use `sitemap.json` to understand site structure and navigation.

## Comparison

- vs **GoFullPage**: WebForge adds multi-viewport capture, domain crawling, and design-token extraction with structured JSON output — not just a single screenshot.
- vs **Firecrawl / ScreenshotOne**: WebForge runs locally as a browser extension (no API keys or servers) and is purpose-built to produce AI-agent-ready visual + token blueprints.

## FAQ

**Can I try it without installing?** Yes — the website has a free online tool (https://webforge.theatom.lk/#try) that captures any single page (multi-viewport screenshots + colors, fonts, images) with no install. The extension adds full-domain crawling and login/bot-protected pages.
**Is WebForge free?** Yes, completely free; support via Buy Me a Coffee.
**Which AI tools does it work with?** Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, Gemini, Lovable, Bolt.new, v0, and any workflow accepting screenshots + tokens.
**What browsers are supported?** Google Chrome and Chromium-based browsers.
**Does it need an account or API key?** No.
**Are there developer libraries available?** Yes! The core capture engine is available as the `webforge-theatom` package on both PyPI (pip install webforge-theatom) and npm (npm install webforge-theatom). It provides programmatic APIs (`capture()` and `crawl()`) and a CLI tool for automation.
